No need to wait.

Sorry I haven't moved this from last week.
I just had some last-minute things come up.
So hopefully what I go through today isn't like, hey, we already know this.
We've been here a week.
Hopefully this is still helpful for you.
As I go through it, feel free to ask me questions throughout.
Certainly open to having this be conversational.
It doesn't have to be just talking at you.
If there's particular things you're curious about, happy to help.
you.

A little bit of context myself, by the way, it's odd being on this side of the house now, where I used to, now I'm leading on the sales side, but previous to this just about a month back, was leading the client services side of the business.
Previous to that, I was the first customer at GrowthX.
I am not a typical salesperson, come from more of a growth background.

I was at a company called Homebase previous to this, still a customer of ours, where I was leading growth about a year ago when he was doing workshops and was so excited, but also was like, I have no time to do this.
At the time, GrowthX wasn't really a thing.
He was teaching people how to build workflows, how to do this for your team, and I was like, yeah, this is really cool.
I have no time for this right now.
Can you please do it for me?
But anyways, that's just a little bit about my background.
Maybe we wanted to go through today was give you a little bit of context about the use cases and work streams, as we call it.
that we serve today.

So happy to give a little bit more context of the why behind that as well.
But again, please feel free to ask me questions throughout.
Bear with me with my super basic three-slide presentation.
It is nothing super fancy.
I really need to add to this.
But a few things we'll go through is just some of our current work streams.
We call them work streams.
They're basically things that we offer to our customers.
We have a variety of potential work streams that we talk about and will continue to evolve that side of the business because that just helps us be stickier, have more upsells, things of that nature.
And we can also look at some specific customer examples.
But...
But...
Let's start here.

You may have seen this slide before, and hopefully I don't just say a bunch of stuff you've already heard before, but, you know, in the early days of when we were, you know, developing GrowthX, it was very exciting, all the opportunities that we saw and still see today.

Like, you know, it's very easy to, like, you know, just get in the room with the right people and just, like, have all these fun ideas of, like, how you can leverage AI automation and build all these really cool workflows and all the different places that can help a go-to-market team or even other places of the business.
But that isn't going to be, like, super helpful if we don't have some level of focus and make sure we can execute things at a high level before we go too broad.
So, you know, that's a bit of the balance that we've been trying to be mindful of in the early goings of GrowthX because, you know...
So, like, if you look at some of the things, say, like Marcelo's doing at DeepGram, and he's doing a lot of things with AI automation beyond just developing content.
He's doing things that was helping the sales team, you know, sales enablement type things, armoring them with signals.
We have customers who will ask about that from time to time.
We sometimes entertain that, but it's just the complexity of those type of use cases is, you know, it's a bit hard to manage, and, like, how would you structure the team?
And so it's something maybe we do in the future, but not the core focus today.

And really, you know, our mindset has been, if we start with the right foundation of developing a content strategy for our customers, then, and do that in really a very effective and intentional way, that should start to open up opportunities to layer in other things.
And so when we say from content,

To distribution, to conversion, that's kind of the idea behind it is, you know, we start with that content strategy, we align on the set of topics that is going to make most sense, that are really going to be effective based off of the personas they care about, the jobs to be done of those personas, what questions that they have associated to those jobs to be done, and how can we create, like, really amazing answers and content that is, all ties back into that kind of framework, and so today, and how we think about developing a content strategy with that in mind, is really starting with editorial content, first and foremost.

We used to, and when I say used to, up until not two, you know, past month or two, you know, have folks that would join us either for editorial content or programmatic content, I'll define those in a second, but
Today, though, we don't want to, and I'll share a little bit about why, but we don't actively sell programmatic today, even though we have some accounts that have programmatic content.
Starting with editorial is really our focus.
We want our customers to operate more like publishers.
That's part of what has informed why we built the team in the way we have.
And again, going back to that, all that I was mentioning before, and like how you start to think about the strategy and build it, and then you start to get that out in the market.
You get signals.
As you get those signals, that should open up a variety of different paths of, do we double down on certain topics?
Do we cut back?
There's so many rich learnings that come from continuing to evolve that side, that it can start to open up, oh, maybe there is a programmatic opportunity here.
Maybe we can help lengthen the distribution of this content and engagement of this audience, layering in things like lifecycle marketing.
Or social media posts and things of that nature.
It's just that we don't want to, it's not like we won't do those things, it's just more we don't want to start engagements today where we would just sell that.
There's an occasional time we'll be flexible if it's like a really amazing brand and like, you know, we're, you know, think that we have the right team to collaborate with there.
We're talking with Agda about something fairly custom, but that's going to be pretty rare for us in this day and age based off our learnings.
So that's, that's a little bit about just how all this, like a bit of the why of some of the shift.
There's a, you know, I can go and give a little bit more context, but just want to make sure that part was, was clear.
You know, I think the other thing to keep in mind is like, why are, why are these businesses coming to us?
So, you know, going back to.

Like why I signed up to be the first customer and, you know, find now being on the sales side, a lot of the conversations are pretty similar in the questions people are having and challenges they're facing.
A lot of my experience lies in like businesses who have reached a certain amount of scale, but are worried or have become too dependent on channels like a paid search.

That is a way that similarly you can capture some of this intent from the content that we may generate, but, and can provide results relatively quickly, but the challenge can be, it gets more, more expensive over time.
So, really, the past handful of years has become a lot of pressure on, especially in the SaaS space of businesses needing to think far more about ROI than they used to.
It's not that this is a relatively new thing, but the pressure to show efficient returns on investment is way higher than it was just five years back.
And with that, then it becomes, it's like, okay, well, I have to continue to show growth in our numbers year on year.

Where paid is getting more expensive, well, that's where an organic can really play nicely in helping them theoretically start to change that dynamic if they have the right strategy, if they have the right scale.

Where a lot of businesses struggle with that, though, is it takes a lot to operationalize that, hire the right team, do all the things that we do here internally, coordinate the right topic, scale that content out.
And so, and why customers come to us is, is the thought that we can help accelerate that dynamic, we can set that right strategy, we can move faster, we can.
The key thing to not lose sight of is at the end of the day, a lot of them are really going to care about those results.
They want this to eventually help them drive more revenue for their business.
It doesn't mean that is going to happen overnight and we try to qualify that and set those expectations early on in our conversations and find businesses that are the right fit for us.

But that's a bit of like why they come to us and why we think about like what is the right foundation to lay and then how does that dynamic shift over time and how can we eventually get them down that path where it like starts to actually convert for them.
So that's just a little bit of a high level of kind of like why we've gone through and adapted our approach a little bit.
I can go into some specific work streams and like where we're at with that and give some more context, some examples.
Yeah.
Before I do, any questions on everything I just ran through there?
I don't what you're talking about.

it's better.

So moving along here a little bit on, in terms of like what we have in active work streams, I kind of mentioned this a little bit already, but really we're starting these engagements now focused on editorial specifically.
And the way we're going about this now is even a bit different.
You know, how a lot of these engagements used to be structured is more often than not, we would be in a 90-day pilot.
The intention of this pilot was for, it helped to somewhat de-risk.
These companies signing up for a 12-month contract and be able to get a sense of what it's like working with us and hopefully being able to see some early results.
It is a bit difficult to show results that quickly, though, in a channel like SEO.

And so, you know, what we found, though, is like, and I won't like just reiterate everything Marcel mentioned during the all hands, but what we found was just like sometimes just like misalignment of expectations.
You know, one of our superpowers, I suppose, if you will, has been like our ability to close deals like very quickly.
But sometimes that's a bad thing if we don't get enough context and really make sure this business is a great fit for us.
So today, you know, we're going to start with these strategy sprints.
That's going to help lay that foundation of like really like going deep on what editorial strategy should be and starting to refine and calibrate on like.
Really great outputs from that strategy and just set the delivery team up better for success on something that is really more defined.
Because really, at the end of the day, having a truly good strategy that is clear and has a roadmap, that is going to be so critical to these engagements.
And it's something that we've done okay at, but I'm really excited.
I think this is going to help us a lot to improve that.
Because if you don't have that visibility into, like, what are you building towards, you're going to hyper-focus on the outputs and maybe even nitpick them even more.
But if you are understanding the path and the journey that you're on, it might look a bit different.
And so, again, that's why we start with editorial.
That's our most mature work stream.
The way we're developing the team is with that highly in mind.
Even how we're developing this, this is, like, a little bit old, honestly.

We're probably going to do away with this, but historically, how we structured this, and you'll probably still see active contracts today that have this represented, is we've typically structured editorial engagements based off of the number of words per month that we'll produce.

You know, essentially, we kind of like work back off of that of like, if, you know, the average article is 1,500 words, you know, this is like a rough range of like, you know, 8 to 15-ish articles a week.
But, you know, this is something we'll, again, we'll likely restructure and do away with, but you'll likely see it in a lot of current engagements.
So, a lot of the process that you'll be trained up on, a lot of the workflows and just process, a lot of it is really with editorial in mind.
So, that's, you know, why we're calling this, you know.
A mature workstream.
Programmatic is another form of content.
If you're not familiar with programmatic, I can show you some examples.
programmatic is kind of a broad term.
can sometimes look and feel like editorial content, but really what programmatic is, is about creating completized pages and scaling out variants that are typically capturing some sort of thing.
Like, a common example of programmatic is, like, there is some sort of intent based off of different locations or different industries.
And so it's like, you know, what is the salary for X role in Chicago and Los Angeles and Boston?
And so, like, you can create all these different pages that in itself, each one of those searches may not have a massive amount of search volume.
Sometimes I can, but in aggregate, you can actually capture quite a bit of volume.
So it's a bit of a different approach.

The reason why we're reworking this, even though we today have many customers who are actively on this work stream, is our process has not just been developed well enough to do this at a high enough level.
You know, how you approach programmatic is a little bit different than editorial.
You know, the editorial bar for programmatic is not going to be the same.
It doesn't mean, you know, we can put out a bunch of crap quality.
It's just, it's not as long a form of content.
It is fairly repeatable in terms of its structure.
It tends to be like fairly, at times, like can be engineering intensive.
And this is just something we haven't been built enough and like having the right workflows that enable us in the right way.

The...

what, what, what, what

a little bit different.

And so we're thinking about kind of like, well.

That looks like, but in the interim, how I talk about this with, how I've talked about this with our customers, how I've talked about this with prospects has been, if we go back to what I was mentioning at the top of like, focusing on the jobs to be done framework, basically, you know, like being really mindful of our personas, the jobs to be done of those people, questions they're going to have associated to some of the challenges and opportunities there and just focus areas.
Those same questions should theoretically be a bit of a window into the type of behaviors we could capture here through a perplexity.
And, you know, if we are then, though, still already doing that and how we're optimizing for SEO, we should be able to show up well in these engines as well.

afirm sorts to, from I've17 yeah,

you.

Because it's still holding, like, most of the things that we know are holding true, but, like, how are you telling people to maybe shift their spending or spend with us?
Yeah, mean, I think it's still, like, it's, I don't know if my, like, answers to that particular side of the question has changed too much in that it's, um...
Like, if you don't invest in organic in some form or fashion, have a true, like, intentional strategy behind that, and you're mostly focused on paid, you're going to regret it at some point.
Now, that doesn't mean paid doesn't have a place.
Like, I actually have a very deep background in paid.
That's where I started my career, on the paid side.
But I've just seen so many times where, like, brands have, like, invested way too late.
My last company, Homebase, was a great example of that.

Like, they had SEO momentum at one point, and then they kind of, like, paused, they took their foot off the gas, they stopped focusing on it, and then it just, like, started to, like, really degrade, and then they struggled to get it back up, and then they struggled to find the right team to help get it back in the right direction, and, like, over a year went by and, like, really affected their growth and their numbers.
The thing about, like, perplexity and, like, it's definitely increasing referral traffic, it's definitely changing the, you know.
Just, you know, landscape of, like, how people are doing their research.
But at the same time, like, you still, like, need content to show up in these places, right?
Now there's a bit of a debate of, like, what type of content is going to be effective.

And then, you know, even Google's doing their own changes to, you know, we didn't have time to go through this today, but encourage people to look into, which I just posted the article in the SEO channel on Slack.
About AI mode, I don't know if any of you have seen this yet in Google, but it's actually, like, quite a big change to how they represent search results.
It's basically, like, much more generative in terms of, like, answering your questions.
And so, like, what does that mean for the future of content?
My long meandering answer to this is, like, I do think, and this has been, you know, true for a bit now.
know, it's been, like, this just puts pressure on...
And teams to really diversify their presence digitally and go back to doing that really well.
That was always a point of good marketing, but now you have to do that even more.

How do you show up in reviews?
What's your social presence like?

They eventually put this on YouTube.
They put it in all these other places.

We're going to ingest the transcript of this, and we are going to...

It even pulls out and attributes quotes.
Like, you know, this is like a good article for someone who's just like, you know, not sure they want to watch the podcast or not, and maybe this gets them to engage.
Hopefully there's SEO value out of each one of these.

Like, they're now on, they have this on YouTube, they now have a blog on it, they can take this article, they can work this into their email nurture streams.
Like, just thinking in that way of like, how do you take and repurpose content and like the distribution of it, like, was always a great point of content and marketing.
And I feel like it's just like all the much more important nowadays for like discoverability and engagement and like, you know, that should lead to more brand mentions organically over time.
brand mentions are definitely one of the list.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Onboard new team members on GrowthX's customer use cases and work streams, providing context on the company's evolving approach to content strategy and AI SEO.](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=72.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is focusing on editorial content as the foundation, starting engagements with strategy sprints to ensure alignment and clear roadmaps](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=830.0)
  - [The company is adapting its approach to meet changing market demands, including concerns about AI search engines impacting traditional SEO](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=1315.0)
  - [GrowthX aims to help customers diversify their digital presence and create content that performs well across various platforms, including AI-powered search](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=1800.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Evolution and Current Focus

  - [Shifted from offering both editorial and programmatic content to prioritizing editorial](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=403.0)
  - [Now starts engagements with strategy sprints instead of 90-day pilots](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=765.0)
  - [Focus on helping customers "operate like publishers" with a strong content foundation](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=436.0)
  - [Aim to accelerate customers' organic growth strategies and eventually drive revenue](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=669.0)

### Work Streams and Offerings

  - [Editorial content: Primary focus, most mature work stream](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=746.0)
  - [Programmatic content: Currently paused for new sales, needs process improvements](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=1098.0)
  - [Link building: Offered for low domain authority sites, partnerships being developed](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=1121.0)
  - [Interview-first long-form content: New offering in beta testing phase](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=1162.0)
  - [Potential future work streams being explored (e.g., AI SEO optimization)](https://fathom.video/share/byWLsb3iWrMLBnzxP2PDYycWNPors3vs?tab=summary&timestamp=1227.0)
